examples.title: A minimal analytical map
examples.summary: Power BI latitude/longitude with a retained canvas marker layer.
examples.expected: The map fits all twelve assets and clicking a point updates the selected count.
examples.limitations: The browser gallery uses synthetic row identities.

examples.title: Inline GeoJSON
examples.summary: A declarative FeatureCollection with no executable content.
examples.expected: Two inline GeoJSON facilities render independently of the Power BI row set.
examples.limitations: Remote GeoJSON additionally requires provider host permission.

examples.title: Simple point symbols
examples.summary: Safe circle symbols with explicit outline and opacity.
examples.expected: Every asset is drawn as a teal circle with a dark outline.
examples.limitations: Large point sets should use clustering or heatmap rendering.

examples.title: Line and polygon styling
examples.summary: Inline operational route and service-area geometry.
examples.expected: A dashed route overlays a translucent service polygon.
examples.limitations: Fill patterns are intentionally partial; solid fills are fully supported.

examples.title: Authored unique values
examples.summary: Categorical status styling with deterministic fallback.
examples.expected: Active, warning, and offline assets use distinct authored symbols.
examples.limitations: Unmapped values use the authored default symbol.

examples.title: Proportional asset value
examples.summary: Data-driven size with a quantitative legend.
examples.expected: Higher-value assets draw larger and the legend shows the size domain.
examples.limitations: Domains are cached from the current resolved layer.

examples.title: Mapped and rotated icons
examples.summary: Built-in icons, categorical mapping, rotation, labels, and badges.
examples.expected: Each category receives a safe built-in icon rotated by heading.
examples.limitations: SVG is sanitized and image icons must be HTTPS or relative.

examples.title: Responsive feature labels
examples.summary: Name labels with zoom and collision controls.
examples.expected: Asset names appear at the authored zoom range.
examples.limitations: Label collision is screen-space based, not cartographic annotation.

examples.title: Safe structured details
examples.summary: Declarative tooltip and popup fields without HTML injection.
examples.expected: Hover reveals a tooltip; click opens synchronized asset details.
examples.limitations: Unrestricted HTML and scripts are never accepted.

examples.title: Filterable categorical legend
examples.summary: Counts, percentages, aggregate values, ordering, search, and isolate.
examples.expected: Legend clicks filter status categories; hover emphasizes matching features.
examples.limitations: Legend search appears when the category list is large.

examples.title: Legend-driven map filtering
examples.summary: A layer-scoped internal filter that retains interaction state.
examples.expected: Selecting a legend category filters only the assets layer.
examples.limitations: It does not replace the Power BI filter pane.

examples.title: Power BI selection pipeline
examples.summary: Internal highlight plus external host selection identities.
examples.expected: Feature and legend selection share the same row identity pipeline.
examples.limitations: The standalone gallery cannot call a Power BI host selection manager.
examples.powerBi: In the visual host, each resolved feature carries its retained Power BI selection identities.

examples.title: Click, visible, invert, and clear
examples.summary: Compact selection control and row/feature counts.
examples.expected: Clicking selects a point; the selection menu can select visible, invert, zoom, or clear.
examples.limitations: Power BI identity submission defaults to a 1,000-identity safety limit.

examples.title: Spatial selection modes
examples.summary: Rectangle, lasso, and geodesic radius tools with modifiers.
examples.expected: Drag any enabled shape; Shift adds, Ctrl/Cmd toggles, and Alt subtracts.
examples.limitations: Selection targets resolved feature geometry, not heatmap pixels.

examples.title: Selectable point clusters
examples.summary: Dense points grouped without unnecessary DOM markers.
examples.expected: Nearby assets group at low zoom and separate as the map zooms in.
examples.limitations: Cluster aggregation supports count or one numeric sum.

examples.title: Weighted canvas heatmap
examples.summary: True intensity pixels with viewport normalization and retained hit points.
examples.expected: A weighted raster updates when filters or external highlights change.
examples.limitations: Pixels are not selectable; optional transparent source points provide hit testing.

examples.title: Viewport density grid
examples.summary: Count aggregation into stable screen-space cells.
examples.expected: Assets aggregate into colored density cells.
examples.limitations: Density cells summarize points and are not editable GIS features.

examples.title: ArcGIS feature query
examples.summary: Viewport-aware service features with service renderer support.
examples.expected: The public service is queried for the current viewport.
examples.limitations: Requires network access and an allowed ArcGIS provider host.

examples.title: ArcGIS cached tiles
examples.summary: Read-only operational tile overlay.
examples.expected: Cached ArcGIS tiles render beneath analytical points.
examples.limitations: Raster tile pixels do not participate in selection.

examples.title: Dynamic service identify
examples.summary: Export-image layer with read-only click identify.
examples.expected: The service image follows the viewport; click identify returns temporary results.
examples.limitations: Identify results never participate in joins or selection.

examples.title: Service-to-model joins
examples.summary: Declarative normalized key matching and aggregate policy.
examples.expected: Service features resolve model fields through a safe join definition.
examples.limitations: This live service example depends on stable public service keys.

examples.title: Layer groups
examples.summary: Authored group visibility, opacity, and order.
examples.expected: The layer panel exposes the Operations group as one authored unit.
examples.limitations: Viewer changes are session state and do not mutate the specification.

examples.title: Viewer layer controls
examples.summary: Layer visibility, opacity, labels, and ordering controls.
examples.expected: The layer panel toggles assets and changes retained layer opacity.
examples.limitations: Only controls explicitly enabled by the author appear.

examples.title: Authored map bookmarks
examples.summary: Home extent, bookmarks, and zoom-to-selection.
examples.expected: Bookmark buttons move between downtown and the full operating area.
examples.limitations: Bookmarks store view only, not complete filter state.

examples.title: Provider-backed location search
examples.summary: User-triggered geocoding with safe result navigation.
examples.expected: The search panel is enabled when a geocoder provider is configured.
examples.limitations: No network request occurs without an enabled provider configuration.

examples.title: In-map analytical filters
examples.summary: Category, range, date, text, null, top N, and filter-to-selected.
examples.expected: The filter panel combines lightweight filters and can clear them atomically.
examples.limitations: Quick filters are local analytical state, not a full filter-pane replacement.

examples.title: Performance-aware rendering
examples.summary: Viewport culling, cached domains, canvas density, and warnings.
examples.expected: The layer uses authored culling and feature-limit controls.
examples.limitations: Threshold warnings are guidance; authors still choose appropriate service queries.

examples.title: Compact responsive tools
examples.summary: A single compact toolbar, responsive panels, scale, and coordinates.
examples.expected: Controls remain usable at narrow widths without covering the map.
examples.limitations: Extremely small containers prioritize map interaction over secondary labels.

examples.title: Operations command map
examples.summary: Rich icons, legends, quick filters, spatial selection, heat, and linked interaction.
examples.expected: Two layers remain synchronized through filtering, selection, hover, and counts.
examples.limitations: The gallery uses small deterministic data; production service limits still apply.
